reason: Verified safe; no under-flagging or known-bad slippage. (1) Scope-exclusion clause (import-hygiene/dead-code/naming/style -> code-quality) only suppresses the advisory 'low' unused-import finding in duplicate-data-layer; it never touches a high. Both bad fixtures detect at high 3/3 in the baseline (duplicate-data-layer r0/r1/r2 each yield >=1 high layering finding; layering-violation high 3/3). block_threshold stays 'high' (no loosening). (2) The secondary-finding-folding clause only deduplicates the transaction-leak symptom into the root layering high; at least one high always survives, so recall at block=high is preserved. (3) good/clean-layering is genuinely clean: all 3 baseline repeats returned [] (r1 actively grepped SessionDep+create_item and still found nothing). SessionDep is validly defined in the template routes/items.py (in tree, out of diff) and post_item correctly delegates to repository create_item(session, payload.name), exactly mirroring the template idiom -- no bait, no dirtiness. (4) No fixture edit makes the bad case ambiguous: bad/duplicate-data-layer is explicitly do-not-touch (the documented Phase-0a hunk-counter false positive). The proposed good/lightweight-inline-handler + bad/heavy-inline-handler are coverage additions for the prompt's untested heavy-inline-handler domain, not loosenings, and the entry correctly sets needs_second_good_fixture=false. The only nit (those two new fixtures are unauthored) is coverage expansion, not a precision/recall regression -- not grounds to refuse shipping. Uncertainty is low, so the default-to-refuted tiebreak does not apply.
